boiled down a reproducer below, I can try to simplify it more, but this is as far as I've gotten.

##### julia 1.10
`dot: 0.311097 seconds (2.51 M allocations: 157.250 MiB, 10.19% gc time, 99.98% compilation time)`

##### julia 1.11
`dot: 0.978774 seconds (13.59 M allocations: 612.654 MiB, 19.87% gc time, 99.99% compilation time)`

##### reproducer

```julia
#=
julia +1.10 --project
julia +1.11 --project
using TestEnv; TestEnv.activate() # if you don't have Test in your env.
using Revise; include("../julia_111_perf_regression_reproducer2.jl")
=#

import ClimaComms
ClimaComms.@import_required_backends
using Test
import Random: seed!
using ClimaCore
import ClimaCore:
    Geometry,
    Domains,
    Meshes,
    Topologies,
    Hypsography,
    Spaces,
    Fields,
    Operators,
    Quadratures
using ClimaCore.MatrixFields

function test_spaces(::Type{FT}) where {FT}
    velem = 20
    helem = npoly = 1
    comms_device = ClimaComms.device()
    comms_ctx = ClimaComms.SingletonCommsContext(comms_device)
    hdomain = Domains.SphereDomain(FT(10))
    hmesh = Meshes.EquiangularCubedSphere(hdomain, helem)
    htopology = Topologies.Topology2D(comms_ctx, hmesh)
    quad = Quadratures.GLL{npoly + 1}()
    hspace = Spaces.SpectralElementSpace2D(htopology, quad)
    vdomain = Domains.IntervalDomain(
        Geometry.ZPoint{FT}(0),
        Geometry.ZPoint{FT}(10);
        boundary_names = (:bottom, :top),
    )
    vmesh = Meshes.IntervalMesh(vdomain, nelems = velem)
    vtopology = Topologies.IntervalTopology(comms_ctx, vmesh)
    vspace = Spaces.CenterFiniteDifferenceSpace(vtopology)
    sfc_coord = Fields.coordinate_field(hspace)
    hypsography = Hypsography.Flat()
    center_space = Spaces.ExtrudedFiniteDifferenceSpace(hspace, vspace, hypsography)
    face_space = Spaces.FaceExtrudedFiniteDifferenceSpace(center_space)

    return center_space, face_space
end

function random_field(::Type{T}, space) where {T}
    FT = Spaces.undertype(space)
    field = Fields.Field(T, space)
    parent(field) .= rand.(FT)
    return field
end

@testset "Matrix Multiplication At Boundaries" begin
    FT = Float64
    center_space, face_space = test_spaces(FT)
    seed!(1) # ensures reproducibility
    ᶜᶜmatrix_with_outside_entries = random_field(TridiagonalMatrixRow{FT}, center_space)
    ᶜᶜmatrix_without_outside_entries = random_field(DiagonalMatrixRow{FT}, center_space)

    @time "dot" @. ᶜᶜmatrix_without_outside_entries ⋅ ᶜᶜmatrix_with_outside_entries
end

Here is an example where printing truncated types takes quite a while with a highly nested struct:

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo1(t::Nested) = error("oops")
foo2(t::Nested) = foo1(t)
foo3(t::Nested) = foo2(t)
foo4(t::Nested) = foo3(t)
foo5(t::Nested) = foo4(t)
foo6(t::Nested) = foo5(t)
foo7(t::Nested) = foo6(t)
foo8(t::Nested) = foo7(t)
foo9(t::Nested) = foo8(t)
foo10(t::Nested) = foo9(t)
NV = nest_val(20); # be careful with changing 20 to larger
foo10(NV) # errors, takes ~45 seconds
```

I haven't looked at the implementation in a while, but I imagine that this could be related to #55807.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

main motivation of this PR is to fix #55807.
dc689fe8700f70f4a4e2dbaaf270f26b87e79e04 tries to remove the slow `may_contain_union_decision` check by re-organizing the code path. Now the fast path has been removed and most of its optimization has been integrated into the preserved slow path.
Since the slow path stores all inner ∃ decisions on the outer most R stack, there might be overflow risk. aee69a41441b4306ba3ee5e845bc96cb45d9b327 should fix that concern.

The reported MWE now becomes
```julia
  0.000002 seconds
  0.000040 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.00% compilation time)
  0.000023 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 49.36% compilation time)
  0.000026 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 50.38% compilation time)
  0.000027 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 54.95% compilation time)
  0.000019 seconds (106 allocations: 4.922 KiB, 49.73% compilation time)
  0.000024 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.24% compilation time)
```

Local bench also shows that 72855cd slightly accelerates `OmniPackage.jl`'s loading
```julia
julia> @time using OmniPackage
# v1.11rc4
 20.525278 seconds (25.36 M allocations: 1.606 GiB, 8.48% gc time, 12.89% compilation time: 77% of which was recompilation)
# v1.11rc4+aee69a4+72855cd 
 19.527871 seconds (24.92 M allocations: 1.593 GiB, 8.88% gc time, 15.13% compilation time: 82% of which was recompilation)
```

##### julia 1.10
`dot: 0.311097 seconds (2.51 M allocations: 157.250 MiB, 10.19% gc time, 99.98% compilation time)`

##### julia 1.11
`dot: 0.978774 seconds (13.59 M allocations: 612.654 MiB, 19.87% gc time, 99.99% compilation time)`

##### reproducer

```julia
#=
julia +1.10 --project
julia +1.11 --project
using TestEnv; TestEnv.activate() # if you don't have Test in your env.
using Revise; include("../julia_111_perf_regression_reproducer2.jl")
=#

import ClimaComms
ClimaComms.@import_required_backends
using Test
import Random: seed!
using ClimaCore
import ClimaCore:
    Geometry,
    Domains,
    Meshes,
    Topologies,
    Hypsography,
    Spaces,
    Fields,
    Operators,
    Quadratures
using ClimaCore.MatrixFields

function test_spaces(::Type{FT}) where {FT}
    velem = 20
    helem = npoly = 1
    comms_device = ClimaComms.device()
    comms_ctx = ClimaComms.SingletonCommsContext(comms_device)
    hdomain = Domains.SphereDomain(FT(10))
    hmesh = Meshes.EquiangularCubedSphere(hdomain, helem)
    htopology = Topologies.Topology2D(comms_ctx, hmesh)
    quad = Quadratures.GLL{npoly + 1}()
    hspace = Spaces.SpectralElementSpace2D(htopology, quad)
    vdomain = Domains.IntervalDomain(
        Geometry.ZPoint{FT}(0),
        Geometry.ZPoint{FT}(10);
        boundary_names = (:bottom, :top),
    )
    vmesh = Meshes.IntervalMesh(vdomain, nelems = velem)
    vtopology = Topologies.IntervalTopology(comms_ctx, vmesh)
    vspace = Spaces.CenterFiniteDifferenceSpace(vtopology)
    sfc_coord = Fields.coordinate_field(hspace)
    hypsography = Hypsography.Flat()
    center_space = Spaces.ExtrudedFiniteDifferenceSpace(hspace, vspace, hypsography)
    face_space = Spaces.FaceExtrudedFiniteDifferenceSpace(center_space)

    return center_space, face_space
end

function random_field(::Type{T}, space) where {T}
    FT = Spaces.undertype(space)
    field = Fields.Field(T, space)
    parent(field) .= rand.(FT)
    return field
end

@testset "Matrix Multiplication At Boundaries" begin
    FT = Float64
    center_space, face_space = test_spaces(FT)
    seed!(1) # ensures reproducibility
    ᶜᶜmatrix_with_outside_entries = random_field(TridiagonalMatrixRow{FT}, center_space)
    ᶜᶜmatrix_without_outside_entries = random_field(DiagonalMatrixRow{FT}, center_space)

    @time "dot" @. ᶜᶜmatrix_without_outside_entries ⋅ ᶜᶜmatrix_with_outside_entries
end

main motivation of this PR is to fix #55807.
dc689fe8700f70f4a4e2dbaaf270f26b87e79e04 tries to remove the slow `may_contain_union_decision` check by re-organizing the code path. Now the fast path has been removed and most of its optimization has been integrated into the preserved slow path.
Since the slow path stores all inner ∃ decisions on the outer most R stack, there might be overflow risk. aee69a41441b4306ba3ee5e845bc96cb45d9b327 should fix that concern.

The reported MWE now becomes
```julia
  0.000002 seconds
  0.000040 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.00% compilation time)
  0.000023 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 49.36% compilation time)
  0.000026 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 50.38% compilation time)
  0.000027 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 54.95% compilation time)
  0.000019 seconds (106 allocations: 4.922 KiB, 49.73% compilation time)
  0.000024 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.24% compilation time)
```

Local bench also shows that 72855cd slightly accelerates `OmniPackage.jl`'s loading
```julia
julia> @time using OmniPackage
# v1.11rc4
 20.525278 seconds (25.36 M allocations: 1.606 GiB, 8.48% gc time, 12.89% compilation time: 77% of which was recompilation)
# v1.11rc4+aee69a4+72855cd 
 19.527871 seconds (24.92 M allocations: 1.593 GiB, 8.88% gc time, 15.13% compilation time: 82% of which was recompilation)
```

dc689fe8700f70f4a4e2dbaaf270f26b87e79e04 tries to remove the slow `may_contain_union_decision` check by re-organizing the code path. Now the fast path has been removed and most of its optimization has been integrated into the preserved slow path.
Since the slow path stores all inner ∃ decisions on the outer most R stack, there might be overflow risk.
aee69a41441b4306ba3ee5e845bc96cb45d9b327 should fix that concern.

The reported MWE now becomes
```julia
  0.000002 seconds
  0.000040 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.00% compilation time)
  0.000023 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 49.36% compilation time)
  0.000026 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 50.38% compilation time)
  0.000027 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 54.95% compilation time)
  0.000019 seconds (106 allocations: 4.922 KiB, 49.73% compilation time)
  0.000024 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.24% compilation time)
```

Local bench also shows that 72855cd slightly accelerates `OmniPackage.jl`'s loading
```julia
julia> @time using OmniPackage
 20.525278 seconds (25.36 M allocations: 1.606 GiB, 8.48% gc time, 12.89% compilation time: 77% of which was recompilation)
 19.527871 seconds (24.92 M allocations: 1.593 GiB, 8.88% gc time, 15.13% compilation time: 82% of which was recompilation)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56157/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56157/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2411232251","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56157#issuecomment-2411232251","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56157","id":2411232251,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PuHv7","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-14T13:12:30Z","updated_at":"2024-10-14T13:12:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> It's best to cherry pick backports with the `-x` option to leave a reference to the original commit.

a outcome from this discourse issue https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-to-define-a-macro-via-package-extension/114218/5
I want to open an issue to track that the syntax to extend functions and macros do not match.

While extending functions and macros works the same when importing them (like `import OtherPackage: @theirmacro`), using qualified definitions only works for functions.

works:
```julia
function OtherPackage.theirfunction(args...)
    # works
end
```
doesn't work:
```julia
macro OtherPackage.theirmacro(args...)
    # fails with `ERROR: syntax: invalid macro definition ...`
end
```

As the functionality of extending is already support for both, it would be great if also the syntax support is Yeah, that's accurate. I put a fair bit of work into removing multiple integer indexing. We'd like to reserve linear indexing (i.e., multiple integer indexing) so that we can special-case fixes for https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28126 (including the GPU-variant related issue resolution efforts: https://github.com/JuliaGPU/GPUArrays.jl/pull/454, https://github.com/JuliaGPU/GPUArrays.jl/pull/464).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2430562785/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

dc689fe8700f70f4a4e2dbaaf270f26b87e79e04 tries to remove the slow `may_contain_union_decision` check by re-organizing the code path. Now the fast path has been removed and most of its optimization has been integrated into the preserved slow path.
Since the slow path stores all inner ∃ decisions on the outer most R stack, there might be overflow risk.
aee69a41441b4306ba3ee5e845bc96cb45d9b327 should fix that concern.

The reported MWE now becomes
```julia

  0.000019 seconds (106 allocations: 4.922 KiB, 49.73% compilation time)
  0.000024 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.24% compilation time)
```

Local bench also shows that 72855cd slightly accelerates `OmniPackage.jl`'s loading
```julia
julia> @time using OmniPackage
 20.525278 seconds (25.36 M allocations: 1.606 GiB, 8.48% gc time, 12.89% compilation time: 77% of which was recompilation)
 19.527871 seconds (24.92 M allocations: 1.593 GiB, 8.88% gc time, 15.13% compilation time: 82% of which was recompilation)
```

Here is a reproducer:

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo(t::Nested) = 1
for i in 1:25
    let i=i
        NV = nest_val(i)
        @time begin
            foo(NV)
        end
    end
end
```

Here is a reproducer:

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo(t::Nested) = 1
for i in 1:25
    let i=i
        NV = nest_val(i)
        @time begin
            foo(NV)
        end
    end
end
```

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo(t::Nested) = 1
for i in 1:20
    let i=i
        local NV
        ts = @elapsed begin
            NV = nest_val(i)
        end
        tc = @elapsed begin
            foo(NV)
        end
        println("make struct, compile foo ($ts, $tc)")
    end
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2359253129/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

think I found a regression with compiling methods with increasing type information (cc @gbaraldi). Here is a reproducer:

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo(t::Nested) = 1
for i in 1:4:25
    let i=i
        NV = nest_val(i)
        @time begin
            foo(NV)
        end
    end
end
```

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-11T02:25:10Z","updated_at":"2024-06-11T02:25:10Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Describe the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

the bug 🐞**

We're seeing that the latest version of SciMLBase requires `ndims` to be defined. I've only just run into this issue. I fixed it in ClimaCore.jl, but this popped up in ClimaTimeSteppers.jl with

```julia
   LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)
│       @ Base abstractarray.jl:276
│      ndims(!Matched::HDF5.Dataspace)
│       @ HDF5 ~/.julia/packages/HDF5/Z859u/src/dataspaces.jl:95
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{<:LazyArrays.Applied{<:Any, typeof(*), Args}}) where Args
│       @ LazyArrays ~/.julia/packages/LazyArrays/JNdsG/src/linalg/mul.jl:41
│      ...
```

**Expected behavior**

I expect that minor releases are non-breaking changes.

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
cd ClimaTimeSteppers.jl
git checkout ck/compat_and_deps
julia --project=docs
include("docs/make.jl")
```

**Error & Stacktrace ⚠️**

```julia
│   exception =
│    LoadError: MethodError: no method matching ndims(::Type{Tuple{Float64, Float64}})
│    
│    Closest candidates are:
│      ndims(!Matched::Type{Union{}}, Any...)

collection"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-07-14T18:02:16Z","updated_at":"2024-03-24T18:22:19Z","closed_at":"2023-07-15T12:40:28Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If `Base.Broadcast.flatten` is called on a `Broadcasted` object with a `DataType` `f`, the resulting `Broadcasted` object is no longer type-stable, triggering allocations.

This can be triggered when using `||` or `&&` in expressions, a simple reproducible example of this:
```julia
julia> x = rand(5); y = rand(5);

julia> f!(y, x) = @. y = ifelse(false || x < Float32(1), x, y)
f! (generic function with 1 method)

julia> f!(y, x);

julia> @allocated f!(y, x)
800
```

I suspect that this will fail altogether on the  CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1480585616","pull_request_review_id":1866392709,"id":1480585616,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YP_GQ","diff_hunk":"@@ -201,6 +205,26 @@ CompoundPeriod(t::Time) = CompoundPeriod(Period[Hour(t), Minute(t), Second(t), M
 
 CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * nf)))","path":"stdlib/Dates/src/periods.jl","commit_id":"b88128bd83cb444bb4dc1768eb5e2253eb4dd76e","original_commit_id":"9860458c96c8d84c95310b25a7f994b4e26ff597","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Ah, I see what you mean. PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1480779072","pull_request_review_id":1866720678,"id":1480779072,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YQuVA","diff_hunk":"@@ -201,6 +205,26 @@ CompoundPeriod(t::Time) = CompoundPeriod(Period[Hour(t), Minute(t), Second(t), M
 
 CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * nf)))","path":"stdlib/Dates/src/periods.jl","commit_id":"b88128bd83cb444bb4dc1768eb5e2253eb4dd76e","original_commit_id":"9860458c96c8d84c95310b25a7f994b4e26ff597","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"`time_per_time` basically duplicates part of the logic in `define_conversions` below, so I assume that it's somewhat irreducible. PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1481873970","pull_request_review_id":1868450943,"id":1481873970,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YU5oy","diff_hunk":"@@ -201,6 +205,26 @@ CompoundPeriod(t::Time) = CompoundPeriod(Period[Hour(t), Minute(t), Second(t), M
 
 CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * nf)))","path":"stdlib/Dates/src/periods.jl","commit_id":"b88128bd83cb444bb4dc1768eb5e2253eb4dd76e","original_commit_id":"9860458c96c8d84c95310b25a7f994b4e26ff597","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"`time_per_time` cannot error, since it returns the number of time units when going from one conversion to another (and that's possible because it's only defined when going from larger periods to smaller periods, and there's no possibility for overflow since it's independent of the value we're working with). As for `value(convert(Nanosecond, T(1)))`, there's no guarantee for this to be exact, and therefore errors in the converts.

We could change the converts below to use `time_per_time`, and define a single convert method instead of eval-ing a bunch:

```
function Base.convert(::Type{T}, x::Tc) where {T <: Period, Tc <: Period}
	N = T === Period ? 1 : time_per_time_new(T, Tc)
	vmax = typemax(Int64) ÷ N
	vmin = typemin(Int64) ÷ N
	vmin ≤ value(x) ≤ vmax || throw(InexactError(:convert, T, x))
	return T(value(x) * N)
end
```
Would this be preferable?

We could apply a similar pattern to extract `N` for the "`# more-precise periods`" conversions / promotions, but I don't know what benefit this might PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

 CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1481964703","pull_request_review_id":1868587878,"id":1481964703,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YVPyf","diff_hunk":"@@ -201,6 +205,26 @@ CompoundPeriod(t::Time) = CompoundPeriod(Period[Hour(t), Minute(t), Second(t), M
 
 CompoundPeriod(p::Period...) = CompoundPeriod(Period[p...])
 
+time_per_time(::Type{P}, ::Type{P}) where {P} = 1
+function define_time_per_times(periods)
+    for i = eachindex(periods)
+        T, n = periods[i]
+        N = Int64(1)
+        for j = (i - 1):-1:firstindex(periods) # less-precise periods
+            Tc, nc = periods[j]
+            N *= nc
+            @eval time_per_time(::Type{$T}, ::Type{$Tc}) = $N
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+define_time_per_times([(:Week, 7), (:Day, 24), (:Hour, 60), (:Minute, 60), (:Second, 1000),
+                    (:Millisecond, 1000), (:Microsecond, 1000), (:Nanosecond, 1)])
+
+function CompoundPeriod(x::Real, ::Type{T}) where {T <: Period}
+    nf = time_per_time(Nanosecond, T)
+    return CompoundPeriod(Nanosecond(ceil(x * nf)))","path":"stdlib/Dates/src/periods.jl","commit_id":"b88128bd83cb444bb4dc1768eb5e2253eb4dd76e","original_commit_id":"9860458c96c8d84c95310b25a7f994b4e26ff597","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Ahhhhh, I see. PR defines conversions, or rather, `CompoundPeriod` constructors that accept `Real` numbers. For example (from the tests added):

```julia
    ccp(x, p) = Dates.canonicalize(Dates.CompoundPeriod(x, p))
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Minute)) == "1 minute, 30 seconds"
    @test string(ccp((Dates.Second(Dates.Week(1))+Dates.Second(1)).value, Dates.Second)) == "1 week, 1 second"
    @test string(ccp(1.5, Dates.Day)) == "1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.5+7, Dates.Day)) == "1 week, 1 day, 12 hours"
    @test string(ccp(1.4, Dates.Second)) == "1 second, 400 milliseconds"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 48 minutes"
    @test string(ccp(10.1, Dates.Week) - Dates.Minute(6)) == "10 weeks, 16 hours, 42 minutes"

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979","id":355787977,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUzNTU3ODc5Nzc=","number":28979,"title":"Can not call @sync from another macro in Julia 0.7","user":{"login":"mattcbro","id":5304586,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUzMDQ1ODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5304586?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro","html_url":"https://github.com/mattcbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2018-08-30T23:21:15Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T20:02:03Z","closed_at":"2022-10-31T04:49:45Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It appears to be impossible to use the `@sync` macro within another macro or conversely call `@sync` on a user defined macro.

Case in point:
```julia
using Distributed
macro parsync(args...)
    :(@sync @distributed $(args...))
end
```
Use of this macro gives:
```
julia> @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
ERROR: syntax: invalid let syntax
Stacktrace:
 [1] eval at ./boot.jl:319 [inlined]
 [2] #337 at /home/matt/.julia/packages/Atom/jodeb/src/repl.jl:129 [inlined]
 [3] with_logstate(::getfield(Main, Symbol("##337#339")), ::Base.CoreLogging.LogState) at ./logging.jl:397
 [4] with_logger(::Function, ::Atom.Progress.JunoProgressLogger) at ./logging.jl:493
 [5] top-level scope at /home/matt/.julia/packages/Atom/jodeb/src/repl.jl:128
```
If I remove the `@sync` from my `parsync` macro,  namely:
```
julia> macro parsync(args...)
           :(@distributed $(args...))
       end;

julia> @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
Task (runnable) @0x00007fb59a78f4903
```
If I use `@sync` outside of my macro it works with the raw `@distributed` macro
```
julia> @sync @distributed for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
3
Task (done) @0x00007fb599774fd0
```
If `@parsync` only calls `@distributed` without the `@sync`, then using `@sync` with `@parsync`  appears to have no effect on `@parsync` namely,
```
julia> @sync @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
Task (runnable) @0x00007fb5997792703
```
Number n=3 is never printed.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979","id":355787977,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUzNTU3ODc5Nzc=","number":28979,"title":"Can not call @sync from another macro in Julia 0.7","user":{"login":"mattcbro","id":5304586,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUzMDQ1ODY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5304586?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro","html_url":"https://github.com/mattcbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattcbro/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2018-08-30T23:21:15Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T20:53:22Z","closed_at":"2022-10-31T04:49:45Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It appears to be impossible to use the `@sync` macro within another macro or conversely call `@sync` on a user defined macro.

Case in point:
```julia
using Distributed
macro parsync(args...)
    :(@sync @distributed $(args...))
end
```
Use of this macro gives:
```
julia> @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
ERROR: syntax: invalid let syntax
Stacktrace:
 [1] eval at ./boot.jl:319 [inlined]
 [2] #337 at /home/matt/.julia/packages/Atom/jodeb/src/repl.jl:129 [inlined]
 [3] with_logstate(::getfield(Main, Symbol("##337#339")), ::Base.CoreLogging.LogState) at ./logging.jl:397
 [4] with_logger(::Function, ::Atom.Progress.JunoProgressLogger) at ./logging.jl:493
 [5] top-level scope at /home/matt/.julia/packages/Atom/jodeb/src/repl.jl:128
```
If I remove the `@sync` from my `parsync` macro,  namely:
```
julia> macro parsync(args...)
           :(@distributed $(args...))
       end;

julia> @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
Task (runnable) @0x00007fb59a78f4903
```
If I use `@sync` outside of my macro it works with the raw `@distributed` macro
```
julia> @sync @distributed for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
3
Task (done) @0x00007fb599774fd0
```
If `@parsync` only calls `@distributed` without the `@sync`, then using `@sync` with `@parsync`  appears to have no effect on `@parsync` namely,
```
julia> @sync @parsync for n=1:3 println("$n") end
1
2
Task (runnable) @0x00007fb5997792703
```
Number n=3 is never printed.

I'm trying to create some universal macros I can use that depend on the version number  of Julia,  but this issue is blocking me for my modules containing parallel tasks.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1756231650","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979#issuecomment-1756231650","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28979","id":1756231650,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5orffi","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-10T20:53:21Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T20:53:21Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The
```julia
macro deferfix(ex)
	esc(:($Base.@sync $Threads.@spawn $(ex)))
end
```
pattern worked, thanks!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1756231650/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

of Julia Tasks' stacks are missed by `@time` and Allocation Profiler, and at least 9 other allocations missed by Allocation Profiler","user":{"login":"NHDaly","id":1582097,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1ODIwOTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1582097?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly","html_url":"https://github.com/NHDaly","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2023-01-10T22:31:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-08T15:58:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There are 9 objects being allocated when allocating a Julia Task that are missed by the Allocation Profiler (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768). 

Additionally, both the Allocation Profiler and `@time` are missing the allocation of the _stack_ for a Julia Task.

You can see in the following example, that we are actually missing around 9 allocations, ~including the allocation of the Task stack,~ but they _are_ tracked by `@time`:
```julia
julia> using Profile

julia> @time Threads.@spawn sleep(10)  # warmup
  0.025083 seconds (23.00 k allocations: 1.436 MiB, 67.28% compilation time)
Task (runnable) @0x000000013056ab60

julia> @time Threads.@spawn sleep(10)  # warmup (again?)
  0.000061 seconds (30 allocations: 2.102 KiB)
Task (runnable) @0x0000000110b73340

julia> @time Threads.@spawn sleep(10)
  0.000064 seconds (29 allocations: 2.070 KiB)
Task (runnable) @0x0000000130568fd0

julia> @time Threads.@spawn sleep(10)
  0.000072 seconds (29 allocations: 2.070 KiB)
Task (runnable) @0x0000000130569270

julia> @time Threads.@spawn sleep(10)
  0.000066 seconds (29 allocations: 2.070 KiB)
Task (runnable) @0x0000000130569510

julia> Profile.Allocs.clear(); begin
           Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 Threads.@spawn sleep(10)
       end
Task (runnable) @0x0000000130569660

julia> [a.size for a in Profile.Allocs.fetch().allocs]
20-element Vector{Int64}:
  48
  56
 104
  80
  16
  32
  56
 200
  32
  56
 128
   7
  40
  64
  89
  64
  32
  16
  32
 328

```

I think the stack allocation happens here, but i'm not 100% sure:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/b7201d6b460df74f024a6f19f837437a2c0613d1/src/gc-stacks.c#L150-L182

or maybe this is a better place to instrument - this is the actual site of the `mmap`:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/b7201d6b460df74f024a6f19f837437a2c0613d1/src/gc-stacks.c#L53-L67

~Where does this get tracked and added to the allocs reported in `@time`?~

EDIT: I believe that the stack allocation is _also_ not being captured by `@time`, since I think they're more like 4MiB, and I don't see any accounting for the allocation in the above working on some custom time steppers in ClimaTimeSteppers, and we've been able to nicely extend things using DiffEqBase (e.g., `DiffEqBase.AbstractODEAlgorithm`) and remain compatible with OrdinaryDiffEq. One thing I realized, however, is that to extend `alg_order`, we either need to do one of the following:

 - Directly depend on OrdinaryDiffEq, which seems a shame since it's not technically needed for for anything except the test suite. This isn't really a problem, except that we're trying to keep our dependencies slim

 - Only overload `OrdinaryDiffEq.alg_order` in the test suite (which is what I'm temporarily leaning towards)

 - Have `alg_order` moved to DiffEqBase. This seems ideal since `AbstractODEAlgorithm` is defined there and that's the only argument to `alg_order`

It doesn't seem like too much work, mind if I open a few PRs?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1825/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1825/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

couldn't find an existing issue, so here goes: it seems that there's inconsistent reporting of allocations between `@allocated` and `Profile.Allocs.@profle`. Here's a MWE:

```julia
import Profile

function foo!(x)
    y = Float64[]
    for i in x
        push!(y, Float64(i))
    end
    return nothing
end

x = zero(1)
foo!(x) # compile
p_allocated = @allocated foo!(x)
@show p_allocated # 144 bytes

Profile.Allocs.clear()
Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate = 1 foo!(x)
results = Profile.Allocs.fetch()
sum(a -> a.size, results.allocs) # 112 bytes
```

```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.8.5
Commit 17cfb8e65ea (2023-01-08 06:45 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin21.5.0)
  CPU: 8 × Apple M1
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-13.0.1 (ORCJIT, apple-m1)
  Threads: 1 on 4 virtual cores
```

to Julia 1.9 `--track-allocation` was one of the only ways to track where allocations where coming from in Julia.
The way it functions is to change the emitted LLVM IR to insert code that tracks allocations occuring.
This often leads to false-positives since these statements interfere with optimization (in particular escape analysis)
and @simonbyrne and I once spend a particular fun couple of days hunting down ghosts.

Since it changes the emitted code it also does not particular play well with package images.

I believe there is no remaining use-case for `--track-allocation` that would not be better served by using the allocation profiler,
and I am proposing to remove of the `--track-allocation` feature in 1.10. 

Are there users  of `--track-allocation` that would object? Are there use-cases where you have fund it particular useful that are not covered by the allocation profiler?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48070/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48070/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1566215301","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48070#issuecomment-1566215301","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48070","id":1566215301,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5dWoyF","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-28T18:06:43Z","updated_at":"2023-05-28T18:06:43Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"What will replace this feature? julia --project=examples

using Pkg; Pkg.update() # update to latest versions

import MPI
function runmpi(file; ntasks = 1)
    MPI.mpiexec() do cmd
        Base.run(
            `$cmd -n $ntasks $(Base.julia_cmd()) --startup-file=no --project=$(Base.active_project()) $file`;
            wait = true,
        )
        true
    end
end

file = joinpath(@__DIR__, "runner.jl")

open(file, "w") do io
    println(io, """using Revise; include("examples/hybrid/cli_options.jl");""")
    println(io, """dict = parsed_args_per_job_id();""")
    println(io, """parsed_args = dict["mpi_baroclinic_wave_rhoe"];""")
    println(io, """include("examples/hybrid/driver.jl")""")
end
runmpi(file, ntasks = 2)

using Pkg; Pkg.update() # update to latest versions

import MPI
function runmpi(file; ntasks = 1)
    MPI.mpiexec() do cmd

        )

end

end

Here's a "MWE", I'll try and trim it down as soon as I can (it currently requires mpi 😞).

Failing build here: https://buildkite.com/clima/climaatmos-ci/builds/6414#0185a74d-41e6-46d9-bff6-46aea45ceb3b/139-198

##### This example works

```julia
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/ClimaAtmos.jl
cd ClimaAtmos.jl/
julia --project=examples
using Pkg; Pkg.add("MPIPreferences"); using MPIPreferences; use_system_binary()
```
Close the session, then:

```julia
julia --project=examples

using Pkg; Pkg.update() # update to latest versions

import MPI
function runmpi(file; ntasks = 1)
    MPI.mpiexec() do cmd
        Base.run(
            `$cmd -n $ntasks $(Base.julia_cmd()) --startup-file=no --project=$(Base.active_project()) $file`;
            wait = true,
        )
        true
    end
end

file = joinpath(@__DIR__, "runner.jl")

open(file, "w") do io
    println(io, """ENV["CLIMACORE_DISTRIBUTED"] = "MPI";""")
    println(io, """using Revise; include("examples/hybrid/cli_options.jl");""")
    println(io, """dict = parsed_args_per_job_id();""")
    println(io, """parsed_args = dict["mpi_baroclinic_wave_rhoe"];""")
    println(io, """include("examples/hybrid/driver.jl")""")
end
runmpi(file, ntasks = 2)

Trimmed MWE:

```julia
import MPI
send_ranks = Cint[pid - 1 for pid in 1:2]
send_reqs = MPI.UnsafeMultiRequest(length(send_ranks))

import OrdinaryDiffEq as ODE
p = (; send_reqs)
alg = ODE.Rosenbrock23()
prob = ODE.ODEProblem((du, u, p ,t) -> nothing, Float64[1], (0, 1), p)
ODE.init(prob, alg; dt = 0.1)
```

DiffEqBase","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-12-30T17:12:55Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T17:12:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We're working on some custom time steppers in ClimaTimeSteppers, and we've been able to nicely extend things using DiffEqBase (e.g., `DiffEqBase.AbstractODEAlgorithm`) and remain compatible with OrdinaryDiffEq. One thing I realized, however, is that to extend `alg_order`, we either need to do one of the following:

 - Directly depend on OrdinaryDiffEq, which seems a shame since it's not technically needed for for anything except the test suite. This isn't really a problem, except that we're trying to keep our dependencies slim

 - Only overload `OrdinaryDiffEq.alg_order` in the test suite (which is what I'm temporarily leaning towards)

 - Have `alg_order` moved to DiffEqBase. This seems ideal since `AbstractODEAlgorithm` is defined there and that's the only argument to `alg_order`

It doesn't seem like too much work, mind if I open a few PRs?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1825/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1825/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

was recently trying to update a function in [TurbulenceConvection.jl](https://github.com/CliMA/TurbulenceConvection.jl), [here](https://github.com/CliMA/TurbulenceConvection.jl/pull/1285). In doing so, I observed that DiffEqBase is stuck in an infinite recursive call when adding a certain struct into DiffEqBase's `p` (I'll try to get a more specific description of this at some point).

For now, here is the stack trace (btw, I didn't know julia truncates highly recursive calls like this!)

For example:

```julia
using BenchmarkTools

foo(a) = 1;
function foo!(a)
    @. a += foo(a)
    return nothing
end;
a = rand(100);
@benchmark foo!($a)

bar(a) where {A} = 1;          # added unused type parameter {A}
function bar!(a) where {B}     # added unused type parameter {B}
    @. a += bar(a)
    return nothing
end;
a = rand(100);
@benchmark bar!($a)
```
Resulting output for no unused type parameter:

```julia
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 998 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  13.638 ns … 160.222 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     14.812 ns               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   15.515 ns ±   4.163 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

   ▆█▇█▂                                                       ▂
  ▆█████▆▅▆▆▅▅▄▃▄▄▃▃▃▁▄▄▄▁▄▄▄▁▃▃▁▃▁▄▅▇██▇▆▇▇▇▆▆▃▄▅▃▄▄▁▃▁▃▃▃▃▃▃ █
  13.6 ns       Histogram: log(frequency) by time      36.7 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

Resulting output with unused type parameter:

```julia
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 10 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  1.571 μs … 80.060 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 90.39%
 Time  (median):     1.633 μs              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   1.743 μs ±  1.271 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  1.15% ±  1.65%

  ▃█▅▆▃                                                      ▁
  █████▇▆▅▅▆▅▅▃▄▄▅▁▃▁▁▄▁▃▃▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▆▇█▇▇▆▆█▆▆▅▃▄▃▃▄▃▃▁▄▄▄▄▁▄▅▆ █
  1.57 μs      Histogram: log(frequency) by time     3.94 μs <

 Memory estimate: 1.56 KiB, allocs estimate: 100.

```

It seems like maybe this should error?

I'm not sure about the best or most compact / reproducible way to report this is. But, until I (intentionally or accidentally) delete `ck/pkg_issue`, this can be reproduced with:

```
git clone https://github.com/CliMA/TurbulenceConvection.jl
cd TurbulenceConvection.jl
git checkout ck/pkg_issue
julia --project
] up
```

`LocalPreferences.toml` now exists in `TurbulenceConvection.jl/`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3014/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3014/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}
